GRAPHITE SAMPLER AND CONTROL ROD MAINTENANCE FACILITY 74

The MSRE is a 7.5-Mwt circulating-fuel reactor in which ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ fuel dissolved in a solution of lithium and beryllium fluorides is pumped around a closed loop at ${1200}^{ \circ }\mathrm{F}$ .
The 5-in. circulating-system piping is joined by mechanical disconnects consisting of the freeze flange developed especially for molten-salt service.
The 1-1/2-in. fill-and-drain system piping is of all-welded construction.
The portable maintenance shield can be placed over and will cover the opening left by the removal of two of the lower shield plugs, a space 4 ft. wide and up to 10 long.

The major components are too large to pass through an opening 4 ft. wide and their removal thus requires that a large portion of the cell top shielding be removed and that remote-handling techniques be used.
2) overlooks the reactor cell, the drain tank cell, and the building interior through shielding windows.
It contains controls for the 30-ton crane, the 10-ton crane, and the monitors for the television system.

section: 2.2 Maintenance Control Room and Facilities for Remote Operation
text: The maintenance control room and facilities for remote operation consist of the remote maintenance control room; 30- and 10-ton cranes operable from the control room; lift tongs for the lower shield blocks for the reactor cell and drain-tank cell; lift fixtures for the reactor vessel, heat exchanger, fuel pump, and drain tanks; and a closed-circuit television system.

section: 2.2 Maintenance Control Room and Facilities for Remote Operation
text: The maintenance control room has 3-1/2-ft-thick concrete walls to provide adequate shielding for any maintenance operation; three windows to provide a view of the cells and building interior; crane, television, and lighting controls; and radiation monitors, ventilation, sanitary facilities and lights.

section: 2.2 Maintenance Control Room and Facilities for Remote Operation
text: Its floor is 10 ft. above the building main floor and its interior dimensions are 14 by 19 ft.

section: 2.2 Maintenance Control Room and Facilities for Remote Operation
text: Equipment access is by a 4 x 4-ft hatch in the ceiling.

section: 2.2 Maintenance Control Room and Facilities for Remote Operation
text: This crane was rereeled for a hook travel from 875 ft 10-1/2 in. to 823 ft 6 in. elevation and is now actually rated at 7-1/2 tons.

section: 2.2 Maintenance Control Room and Facilities for Remote Operation
text: The 30-ton crane has five speeds in each direction controllable from the cab and three speeds in each direction controllable from a dial and toggle-switch console in the maintenance control room.

Figures 5 through 8 show various views of this equipment.
The slide is a slab of shielding whose dimensions are 12 in. thick by 5 ft wide and either 8-1/2 or 13 ft long depending on the usage.
The slide includes a circular cutout that accommodates a 35-in.-diam. geared plug.
This module, which is assembled next to the slide, contains a 22-in. diameter rotatable-plug mounted eccentrically within a 50-in.-diam. plug.
This is sometimes called the "split section of the rotating plug." All modules and plugs are 12 in. thick and fabricated from carbon steel.
After the frame is installed over a pair of lower shield blocks, the slide is moved to an end track to permit the removal of the blocks, and an opening 52 by 127 in. is provided.
A 3/4-hp motor and gearbox combination mounted on the frame moves the slide through a rack-and-pinion arrangement.
The rate of travel is about 2 in./sec.
A shielding thickness of 12 in. of steel is provided throughout (slides, modules, and frame sides and ends).
This shielding is sufficient to limit radiation levels 24 hr following reactor shutdown to about 10 mr/hr.
Some of these contain 150-w lighting fixtures.
One 8-in.-diam. and one 5-1/2 in.-diam. lead-glass window can be mounted simultaneously for direct viewing.

This sequence is responsible for the rather lengthy procedure required to remove a defective gasket in freeze flange FF 100, and must be taken into account whenever planning a specific maintenance task.
9, consists of the flange body containing the gasket groove, the gasket assembly, the clamps, the guide rods, the disassembly beams, and the lifting eyes.
The gasket loading force is derived from the strain in the horseshoe section of the clamp, which is sprung open a nominal 0.100 in. when the clamp is forced onto the flange.
Forces encountered in seating the clamp on assembly and disassembly operations range from 16 to 34 tons.
For example, under the piping at each flange there is a 5-ton worm-gear screw jack that terminates in a remotely operable hex head, complete with hook, supports, and drive trains.
As mentioned above, the horseshoe-shaped section of a fully seated clamp is sprung open a nominal 0.100 in. to produce a gasket load of approximately 200,000 lb.
The force required to seat a clamp ranges from 16 to 34 tons and is applied remotely by the pair of hydraulic clamp operator tools shown in Fig.
Each consists of a long mast carrying a 20-ton 13-1/2 in. stroke hydraulic ram.
The pin has 0.045 in. clearance inside a hole tapped in the flange at a corresponding location.
The cover is shown on drawing MSRE-SK-D-263.
The covers require 4 in. of clearance
For freeze flange FF-200 a special offset tool is required because the open flange is under a shield support beam.

The pump bowl assembly consists of the pump tank, the volute, the suction and discharge lines from freeze flanges FF-100 to FF-101, the furnace, the support plates, and a number of flanges for disconnecting the pump auxiliary systems.
These components are the graphite-Hastelloy N surveillance samples, the 2-in. removable graphite stringers, and the control rods.
(This program has been described in detail.) Figure 13 shows features of the sample assembly and its location in the core.

This sample assembly, which is roughly a 5-ft-long 2-in.-diam. cylinder, consists of an array of graphite bars, Hastelloy N tensile specimens, and flux-measuring wire inside a basket of perforated sheet metal.
A 48-in.-wide lower shield block, which has a $39 - 1 / 2$ -in.-diam. hole over the center of the reactor vessel.
During reactor operation a filler plug is installed in the $39 - 1 / 2$ -in.-diam. hole.
A 20-in.-diam. standpipe attached to the bottom of the 48-in. shield block by means of a flange and a bolting ring and to the 2-1/2-in. reactor access flange.
A cylinder 36-in. high by $47 - 1 / 2$ -in. in diameter with 4-in. thick walls (of canned-lead construction), which is installed when preparing to take a sample.
A geared work plug, 38-in. diam. by 10-1/2-in. thick, is inserted into the work shield (item 3 above).

When items 1 through 4 are assembled, a sealed compartment is formed above the reactor, through which personnel operating long-handled tools, can open the reactor and withdraw a radioactive sample.
The container is pulled upward out of the standpipe into a carrier with 6-in.-thick shield walls for its trip to the hot cell.

However, access to remove them is gained only after the removal of the control rods and drive housings, the substitution of a 36-in.-diam. standpipe for the 20-in. standpipe, and finally the removal of the control-rod thimble assembly.
Figure 16 shows the top view of the three control rod drive housings and the 20-in. standpipe.

The technique, as developed for the MSRE, requires a permanent 2-by 3-ft base plate located at each possible future joint, four tools that can be placed and operated through the maintenance shield, and a 4200-cps 30-kw induction generator.
Figure 17 shows an unassembled joint and a section of a brazed joint.
The two are pressed together while being heated to $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to form the completed joint.
Figure 18 is the positioning vise suspended from its lifting bail.
Figure 19 shows the pipe cutter in operation.
A $6^{\circ}$ included-angle taper l-in. long is machined on the pipe.
22 and the inert gas purge is begun.
After an appropriate purge period, the induction generator is turned on and the joint is heated to $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in about 2 min.
Axial pressure is maintained on the joint with the positioning vise during heating, and at about $1750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the braze metal melts and the pipe stub travels about $1/8$ -in. further in.
This reduces the joint thickness from 0.005 to less than 0.001 in.
The joint is held at $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for about one minute to assure complete melting and wetting and then permitted to cool.
If the joint does not pass inspection, the furnace can be closed, the joint heated to $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and separated.
A shell of 8-in. of lead has been built around the equipment for shielding during reactor operation.
The maximum source strength can be assumed to be no more than that of 2 radioactive samples of salt.

(Presumably one sample would be associated with the equipment failure and the other due to the accumulation of deposits and spills from the routine handling of many samples.) A radiation level of $400\mathrm{r / hr}$ at 12 in. has been estimated for this.

Thus a movement of 1/16 in. is required to gain clearance to move the clamp.

text: 3.4.4.2 Installing a New Gasket
There is engagement between rotary element and motor for the first 7 in. of this travel.
The lowest part of the impeller does not clear the pump flange until after the first 22-1/2 in. of travel, so the lifting must be done slowly and carefully.
The last 22-1/2 in. of travel must be slow, since the impeller enters the bore of the pump bowl flange at this point.
At the start of the last 7 in. of travel, pins in the motor housing engage a slotted cone on top of the rotary element to provide radial guidance.
At the last 2 in. of travel the mating parts of the splined coupling engage.

3.7 Procedure for Brazing a Joint
Ajax 3200-cps 30-kw induction generator
25-ft induction heating leads
60 or better on the blade.
Place the air motor on the taperer drive, set the pressure to 25 psi, and turn on the air motor.
The operation should take 2 to 3 hr.
After the feed has advanced 5 l/2 turns, the cut is complete.
Let the air motor run 10 min with no further feeding to obtain a smooth finish.
Set the capacitor control to 15.

Start the machine, bring it up to about 5 kw to heat the joint to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and then adjust the power to hold the joint at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ while purging for 1 hr to bake out and off-gas the system.
Adjust the capacitor control if necessary to obtain a power factor of one at 5 kw.

While one operator maintains pressure on the positioning vise drive, the second operator raises the machine power to 60 amp, which results in readings of approximately $140\mathrm{~V}$ and $11\mathrm{~kw}$ .

The joint heats to $1750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in about $1\mathrm{~l} / 2$ min, at which time the vise operator drives the stub in $1/8$ in. or about three-fourths turn and again seats the stub firmly in the fitting.

The machine operator watches the temperature indicator carefully, and when the temperature reaches $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , he immediately cuts the power to 3 to $4\mathrm{~kw}$ and carefully adjusts it to hold $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for $1\mathrm{~min}$ .
Attach the 20-ft lift bail to the chain hoist and to the vise.
Lower the cutter and guide it onto the mounting plate (the plate is $3^{\circ}$ off vertical).
Cut the pipe following the procedure in paragraphs 2 c. through 2 e, and remove the cutter as in paragraph 2 g.
Proceed with the braze as in steps 7 and 8 with the generator power settings obtained in the test braze.

section: 3.8 Procedures for Maintenance of the Sampler-Enricher
text: The specific parts are (1) the inner compartment, (2) the manipulator arm assembly, (3) the removal seal assembly, (4) the light bulb, (5) the maintenance valve, (6) the operational valve, (7) the hot vacuum pump, (8) the containment housing, and (9) the spool piece connecting the pump bowl with the sampler.

section: 3.8 Procedures for Maintenance of the Sampler-Enricher
text: Except for items 5, 6, and 8, these items are independent and can be removed from the assembly by themselves.

section: 3.8 Procedures for Maintenance of the Sampler-Enricher
text: Item 5, the maintenance valve cannot be removed without first removing items 1 and 6.

section: 3.8 Procedures for Maintenance of the Sampler-Enricher
text: Similarly, item 6 cannot be removed without removal of 1.

section: 3.8 Procedures for Maintenance of the Sampler-Enricher
text: While the reactor is operating, approximately 8 in. of lead shielding covers the unit.

section: 3.8 Procedures for Maintenance of the Sampler-Enricher
text: Item 9, the spool piece, however, is an exception because it is located within the reactor shield; the portable maintenance shield and long-handled tools are used for maintaining it.
